text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Deep Research lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Deep Research tools autonomously plan multi-step research tasks, gather sources, and draft citation-based reports for expert review.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Deep Research tools autonomously plan multi-step research tasks, gather sources, and draft citation-based reports for expert review.

text: Deep Research tools can make literature scans and policy reviews faster, but public health departments still need defensible evidence standards. A generated synthesis is only useful if sources are current, relevant, independently verifiable,.

Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.

By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Deep Research training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 13 and Tools 2, 48.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Deep Research tools can help public health departments move faster when they need a structured scan of literature, policies, funding requirements, legal materials, program models, or emerging issues. These tools are best treated as research assistants that produce a starting synthesis. Staff still need to verify sources, check dates, resolve conflicting evidence, and decide what applies to the department's context.

text: Search and summarize recent evidence on outbreaks, environmental risks, interventions, or clinical guidance with citations for expert validation.

text: Compare state, local, federal, or international policies to identify common provisions, gaps, and options for agency consideration.

Use this slide in the Deep Research training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 13 and Tools 2, 48.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

What source types are acceptable for the decision being supported? Who verifies citations, dates, jurisdictional relevance, and evidence strength? How will the department distinguish evidence, implementation examples, vendor claims, and.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI-assisted research brief template, Source verification checklist, Policy or literature scan protocol.
